channel_name: The Compound and Friends · Josh Brown
video_title: Preparing for the Next Capital Loss Cycle | TCAF
gist: PIMCO Group CIO Dan Iverson argues we are entering a credit loss cycle driven by AI disruption of old-economy businesses — independent of the economic cycle — while simultaneously seeing a once-in-a-generation fixed income opportunity as hyperscalers tap bond markets at scale; he frames the macro backdrop as 'rupture and resilience' with high equity valuations, geopolitical fragmentation, and AI spending of nearly $14 trillion over five years creating both risk and return.

themes.theme: Credit Loss Cycle & Private Credit Risk

insights.claim: A credit loss cycle is now underway, driven primarily by AI disruption of old-economy businesses — not a recession — meaning losses will rise even if GDP growth holds up
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Investors in below-investment-grade credit cannot rely on economic resilience to protect them from defaults; AI-driven disruption creates a new, cycle-independent loss vector
insights.quote: that time has arrived you're going to see higher realized losses than we've seen in quite some time and those losses are going to be somewhat independent of the strength of the economy

insights.claim: The more productive AI is at the macro level, the more disruptive it must be at the firm level — productivity gains and credit losses are two sides of the same coin
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: A bullish AI macro view does not protect credit portfolios; investors who are long AI equities AND unhedged below-IG credit face a compound risk
insights.quote: the more productive AI is at the economy level, the more disruptive it's going to be. It's almost necessary

insights.claim: The bad vintages in leveraged/private credit are those from the COVID-era where spreads were extremely tight, lending terms were weak (cov-lite), and money poured in chasing yield — those loans are most exposed to the current loss cycle
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Investors should scrutinize which vintage of private credit or direct lending they hold; 2020-2022 issuance with tight spreads and poor docs is the highest-risk cohort

insights.claim: Actual losses in risky credit have been steadily rising but have been obscured by extend-and-pretend tactics: PIK interest, maturity extensions, and generous refinancing driven by massive inflows into the asset class
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Reported loss rates dramatically understate true credit impairment; when inflows slow or reverse, extend-and-pretend ends and realized losses will accelerate
insights.quote: we'll give you an IOU. We'll pay you later when we can. Um or we'll extend the maturity. So when you have inflows coming in as a portfolio manager, you're feeling more generous

insights.claim: Expected returns for unlevered risky credit (mid-market direct lending, broadly syndicated loans) will compress from ~8% to ~4-5% as loss rates migrate from near-zero to mid-single digits, eroding the spread advantage over safer alternatives
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: A 4-5% net return from risky credit no longer offers meaningful premium over 4.5% Treasuries or 7-8% high-quality structured credit; the risk/reward in below-IG credit has deteriorated significantly
insights.quote: that old 8% type return that you were getting back when high quality bonds were returning one or two is probably going to be four or 5% for a while

insights.claim: The illusion of safety in private credit was created by a long absence of loss cycles — investors confuse low historical volatility with low risk, particularly in assets with infrequent mark-to-market
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Smoothed NAVs and a decade without defaults have trained investors to classify risky credit as 'safe' — the reclassification back to 'risky' will be painful
insights.quote: when you've had a period where we haven't had a loss cycle in a long time, you can create the illusion of safety just because those types of assets haven't moved around a lot in the past

insights.claim: AI disruption is targeting businesses that appeared safest to credit investors: companies with high current cash flows, stable moats, and predictable revenues — lawyers, financial services, software, traditional professional services
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: The 'safe' attributes lenders used to underwrite old-economy businesses are exactly the characteristics AI is eroding; lenders who screened for stable cash flows may have selected into the highest AI disruption risk
insights.quote: businesses that had the so-called boats, they had attractive current cash flows...services software lawyers financial services software all of these areas of the market that are likely to be disrupted

insights.claim: A stagflationary shock — elevated short rates combined with AI disruption — would be dramatically worse for credit than a clean recession, because most leveraged credit is floating rate and already faces structural headwinds
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Floating-rate credit instruments have no duration buffer in a stagflationary scenario: higher rates increase debt service burden at the same time AI reduces cash flow; this is the tail-risk scenario to hedge
insights.quote: you have companies that are facing AI disruption they already have a lot of debt and higher cost to service that debt. So any type of growth shock with inflation remaining elevated would be a far worse scenario for credit

themes.theme: AI Infrastructure Spending & Fixed Income Opportunity

insights.claim: AI infrastructure spending of $7.6 trillion over 5 years is the GDP equivalent of Japan and France combined; total capex across AI, energy/grid, defense, and reshoring amounts to roughly $14 trillion
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: This is the largest planned capital investment cycle in modern history, creating unprecedented bond issuance and structured credit opportunities for active fixed income managers

insights.claim: Hyperscalers that previously generated so much cash they never needed bond markets are now issuing at scale: Nvidia raised $20-30B, Google did first equity offering since 2001 ($10B), Oracle announced $40B (equity + debt); PIMCO has 10-20 deals in pipeline ranging $20-40B each
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: First time in years that bond investors can negotiate terms on hyperscaler-backed deals; the supply/demand dynamic has shifted from issuer-friendly to investor-friendly at the margin
insights.quote: even Nvidia generating the most cash flow of all is still looking to lock in...I saw it take down 20 30 billion

insights.claim: PIMCO can structure AI data center debt to achieve investment-grade credit risk equivalent to Meta while generating spreads 200 basis points above Meta's own bonds — effectively earning high-yield returns on IG-equivalent credit risk
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: For investors who want AI exposure without equity binary risk, structured IG data center credit is the risk/reward sweet spot: Meta-equivalent default risk at HY pricing
insights.quote: you can take advantage of owning Meta Risk...at a spread pickup to their underlying credit of two percentage points...you're talking about getting the same type of yield you'd get on typically a very high risk investment for something that's a bit more complex

insights.claim: The riskier tier of AI lending (lower-quality data center/cloud deals) targets 9-15% returns and should be underwritten as an equity alternative vs NASDAQ stocks — not as fixed income — because the downside resembles equity not senior debt
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Advisors building model portfolios should bucket high-yield AI infrastructure lending alongside equities for risk budgeting, not alongside investment-grade bonds
insights.quote: we'll look at that risk as a equity alternative so we'll compare it to a NASDAQ stock or we'll compare it to a dividend producing equity investment

insights.claim: Data center bond collateral is worth substantially less in a distressed sale than at underwriting — lenders must verify the legal ability to seize and realize on collateral before committing, not assume the nominal asset value
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Loan-to-value ratios on data center financing are meaningfully overstated unless the lender has negotiated strong docs and tested the actual liquidation path; due diligence on doc quality is critical
insights.quote: you better make sure you have the ability to go get it and realize on it and then when you have to go get it the price is a lot lower on that collateral than you tend to think

insights.claim: Correlation between AI capex and energy infrastructure investment means portfolios heavily exposed to tech/data centers also carry concentrated energy/materials risk — these 'four drivers' of spending move together
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Investors who own hyperscaler equity AND data center debt AND energy infrastructure may have much higher factor concentration than realized; stress-test correlation assumptions
insights.quote: you know you got to figure a way to keep the data centers or the manufacturing facilities on. So when you start looking at portfolios, it's pretty important to think about how that risk may move together

insights.claim: High-quality bonds have returned roughly 2.5% annualized over the past 10-15 years, which after ~2% inflation left real returns near zero — the math is dramatically more favorable today with starting yields at 5%+ on the Bloomberg Aggregate
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: The multi-decade argument that bonds were a 'return-free risk' asset has reversed; locking in 7-8% on high-quality credit now offers equity-like expected returns with less volatility
insights.quote: last 10 or 15 years, high quality bonds have generated returns of about 2 and a half%. Take away two-ish% inflation. Nothing there. The math is much more favorable today

insights.claim: Starting yield explains ~95% of the variance in 5-year forward bond returns — investors should expect close to the current starting yield, not more, not less, absent active management alpha
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: With the Bloomberg Aggregate at ~5%, 5-year bond return expectations should be anchored near 5% — any portfolio projecting dramatically more or less is making an active bet on rate levels
insights.quote: 95-ish% correlation between starting yield and 5-year forward returns...almost set your watch by it

insights.claim: US equity risk premium is near zero by Shiller CAPE analysis — stocks are as expensive relative to bonds as they were in the run-up to the dot-com bubble
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Historical precedent shows that when starting equity valuations are this stretched, subsequent 5-10 year real returns are negative or deeply subpar; while this is not a trading signal, it argues for shifting the marginal dollar toward fixed income
insights.quote: it's very very expensive and they're near the most expensive they've been relative to bonds in many many years...those were the years leading up to the internet bubble bursting

insights.claim: PIMCO's positioning favors up-in-quality risk taking — owning higher-quality bonds that generate near-HY returns today vs. going defensively into cash or low-yield sovereigns, given constructive base case on growth
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: The current fixed income opportunity is not a hide-in-cash trade; it is a move up the quality spectrum while capturing spread via structured credit and IG paper — not a defensive posture
insights.quote: now is not the time for wildly defensive...be up in quality or take advantage of this exciting global opportunity set of higher quality risks that can generate similar returns to the real risky stuff

themes.theme: Geopolitics, Macro Regime Shift & PIMCO Secular Outlook

insights.claim: PIMCO's 2026 secular outlook is titled 'Rupture and Resilience' — a deliberate escalation from 2025's 'Fragmentation Era,' signaling that geopolitical and institutional breaks are now more severe and potentially irreversible in the near term
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Investors should not expect mean-reversion to the 2010s globalization equilibrium; the new baseline is higher structural uncertainty, more frequent shocks, and wider range of outcomes

insights.claim: Tariffs represent the clearest example of politics overriding economic efficiency — and are sticky across administrations: Trump's first-term tariffs were not reversed under Biden, suggesting durable trade policy change regardless of who wins in 2028
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Companies and investors who are pricing in tariff reversal are likely wrong; supply chain reshoring and industrial policy are bipartisan in practice even if not in rhetoric
insights.quote: tariffs aren't there just out of purely economic fairness or efficiency. They're as Trump has said the textbooks say tariffs are bad. We're doing them anyway because there's a political reason to do them

insights.claim: Direct stimulus checks during COVID — combined with simultaneous monetary accommodation — were the specific mechanism that broke the pre-COVID low-inflation equilibrium; prior cycles of low growth used stimulus without the same inflationary effect because it went through intermediaries
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Future fiscal responses that involve direct cash transfers to households — UBI experiments, recession stimulus — carry much higher inflation risk than traditional government spending programs
insights.quote: turns out all they had to do to create inflation was mail everyone checks...until they literally couldn't find places to spend it

insights.claim: Iran-related oil disruption through the Strait of Hormuz was PIMCO's biggest macro risk for the remainder of the year; resolution of that conflict would make them 'pretty bullish' on the US economy into year-end
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: The single most important macro watchpoint for H2 2026 is Iranian oil supply normalization — a clean resolution unlocks a bullish macro call; failure to resolve is the base scenario that keeps the Fed on hold
insights.quote: our biggest concern for the remainder of the year was the Iranian situation just classic energy shock

insights.claim: A geopolitical shock (Iran/Middle East war) immediately repriced the front end of the yield curve without any Fed communication — demonstrating that markets do not need Fed guidance to price in inflation/rate scenarios
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Overweighting Fed communication in rate forecasting is a mistake; geopolitical events, fiscal surprises, and energy shocks can move rates faster and more durably than any forward guidance
insights.quote: it didn't take a single Fed speech to get the front end of the yield curve to go higher because now people knew inflation was higher...we didn't need a speech

insights.claim: White-collar professional workers — accountants, lawyers, financial analysts, software developers — face structurally higher unemployment risk than blue-collar workers from AI, reversing the historic pattern where automation displaced manual labor first
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Consumer spending models built on the resilience of the professional middle class need to be stress-tested; precautionary savings could rise significantly among the highest-earning households, dampening consumption
insights.quote: white collar unemployment...the middle income professional cohort group could see more uncertainty than they've experienced in quite some time

insights.claim: Tech sector layoffs reached 150,000 in the year to date — 40,000 in the most recent month alone — happening simultaneously with the biggest NASDAQ bull run in 30 years, suggesting AI-driven efficiency is already displacing workers at scale
insights.speaker: Josh Brown
insights.so_what: The decoupling of stock market performance from tech employment is a leading indicator of broader white-collar displacement; NASDAQ highs do not protect workers in AI-exposed roles
insights.quote: we had 150,000 layoffs in tech this year...40,000 of those were last month. So the pace of layoffs within tech is actually rising in the biggest bull market for the NASDAQ in 30 years

insights.claim: AI-driven white-collar displacement could trigger a regulatory backlash and political reaction against tech companies, creating a new source of tail risk for hyperscaler equities — similar to how globalization's losers eventually produced Trump tariffs
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Investors in hyperscaler equities need to model political/regulatory risk more seriously; the playbook from globalization suggests a multi-year lag between disruption and political reaction, but it does arrive
insights.quote: could lead to more political uncertainty, the risk of regulatory backlash, and a lot of tension

insights.claim: AI will likely be disinflationary over a 5-10 year horizon by driving down the cost of providing goods and services — but inflationary in the near term as massive materials and energy demand from the buildout outpaces supply
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Investors pricing AI as a clean deflationary force are too early; the near-term commodity and energy demand surge from $14T in capex spending keeps inflation elevated for several more years before the productivity benefits dominate
insights.quote: over the next several years we do think that AI could be a gamechanger on the productivity side...but inflationary noise the next few years as you go out there to find materials to build this stuff

insights.claim: PIMCO is actively shifting its hiring mix away from traditional finance/spreadsheet analysts toward technologists who can leverage AI tools, and empowering younger employees who natively understand the technology
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: If the world's largest active fixed income manager is restructuring skill requirements toward AI, every financial services firm will follow — the shelf life of pure fundamental finance skills is shortening rapidly
insights.quote: where you may have had 200 new folks coming in with more traditional finance backgrounds...spreadsheet, discounted cash flow analysis people, probably need less of them

insights.claim: Iverson believes the overwhelming volume of Fed communication — press conferences, dot plots, governor speeches — adds noise rather than signal; Greenspan's intentional opacity produced well-functioning capital markets without the current media circus
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: The marginal information value of Fed communication is declining; sophisticated fixed income managers are looking through the noise rather than trading on speeches, suggesting retail investors overweight Fed communication in their decision-making
insights.quote: I liked Greenspan. He would come out once a year and speak in riddles like the caterpillar from Alice in Wonderland. Nobody had any idea what he said. It was by design

insights.claim: PIMCO expects the new Fed leadership (Waller) to be sufficiently independent on the two things that matter — policy rate and balance sheet — but to prefer clearer, more rules-based and less frequent communication vs the Powell era
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: A Waller Fed that is more rules-based and less activist removes a source of market volatility; fixed income investors should not expect the same level of forward guidance 'insurance' they received under Powell
insights.quote: this is a Fed that would like to have the opportunity to get rates lower even before inflation makes meaningful progress towards their target. But we don't think that's super different than Powell

insights.claim: PIMCO's secular forum — a week-long annual off-site stepping back from daily noise to focus on 3-5 year macro themes — has been held for over 40 years and was supplemented in 2026 with a separate AI speaker series because the topic was too large to cover in one week
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: PIMCO's formal process for separating long-cycle analysis from daily noise is a replicable framework for any investor: designate structured time away from market feeds to think on a 3-5 year horizon

insights.claim: Post-GFC, the era of single large firmwide macro calls (like buying everything housing-related in 2009) gave way to a portfolio of many smaller specialist bets because the big macro dislocations became rarer and markets more technical
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: Active fixed income alpha is now generated through bottom-up credit work and structuring expertise rather than top-down macro calls — pure macro bond managers are structurally disadvantaged vs team-based specialists
insights.quote: years leading up to the financial crisis there was a big trade to do...as you progressed further away from the global financial crisis, yields were low, volatility was low...you almost needed a lot of small things to go well

insights.claim: PIMCO's ability to negotiate terms on AI infrastructure deals — rather than just price — is a core competitive moat because scale allows them to drive doc quality that protects against PIK creep, collateral dilution, and lien subordination in distress
insights.speaker: Dan Iverson
insights.so_what: In a crowded private credit market, the ability to set terms matters more than getting a few extra basis points of spread; smaller managers who take 'market terms' on AI infrastructure deals are accepting risks they likely cannot model
insights.quote: Munger or Buffett...you'd make the price, I'll set the terms...I think in this environment, the terms matter a lot
